speaker: interviewer
text: Or else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark, are you going to sit down there?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, he's going to sit right here. Mira, we're joined by your youngest son, Mark Shelub. Mark, thank you for joining us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you heard your mother's story before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Off and on.

speaker: interviewer
text: In bits and pieces?

speaker: interviewee
text: In bits and pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you growing up with a mother whose background was very different from the rest of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, I didn't become aware of background which was different from the background of the parents of my friends until later on. I had thought that everybody's parents were in Europe and had gone through the war. And I didn't think that that was any different than what anybody else had experienced, my parents' experiences, until I came closer to the age of discretion. Then I understood that it was quite a bit different.

speaker: interviewer
text: What age is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, it was so different that it was-- became indescribable to the extent that I-- I came to understand that I really couldn't understand what they had been through.

speaker: interviewer
text: What age was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know, teens. I mean, it's been a-- it's been a process of understanding more and more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect do you think that had on you overall?

speaker: interviewee
text: The effect of what? Understanding more?

speaker: interviewer
text: Realizing what that meant and what your parents' experience really was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, theirs was a unique experience, and I'm sure that it had effects on me, different-- others of my age hadn't-- have not had those effects because of their background being entirely different from the background of my parents. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Some examples would be [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that they came out of World War II with a terrific sense of economic insecurity. And this was a theme which, consciously or unconsciously, rubbed off on us so that we were highly inspired to enter into some profession or some line of work which would allow us a stable means of livelihood. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I would say also that it made me much more-- it made me very inquisitive as to my background, not only having to do with the Holocaust, but having to do with the meaning of Jewish identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does it mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Jewish identity? Well, that's a very big question. I mean, that has anything to do with my nationality, to my religious beliefs, to my purpose in life. I guess what I meant to say was that search, my interest in my Jewish background, probably came to some extent from the experience of my parents. But that means so many things. I spent time in Israel. It has to do with having a national identity closely bound up with Israel. My religious beliefs [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] having to do with-- having to do with the Torah and what it says, what it expects of me, there's many things, many things involved here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, I want to thank you very much for being with us and joining your mother. I know she's very proud of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think we'll take a break now. We're going to show some photographs of your family and the other members.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, Chaim Michel Raznov. This picture was taken in Navahrudak, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I believe that he was here about the early '60s.

speaker: interviewer
text: So approximately what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was taken in [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in '47-- 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my husband, my brother, my sister, and me. My husband is Nochem Scelubski.

speaker: interviewer
text: From left to right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am--

speaker: interviewer
text: From left to right.

speaker: interviewee
text: From left or right. I am the first one, Mira Scelubski, then Nochem Scelubski, my husband. Then my sister, Sarah Raznov, and my brother, Moris Raznov. This picture was taking-- was taken in 1946 in Bad Gastein, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from left to right of my uncle's son, Ralph, my uncle, my father's brother, Aizik [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Raznov, then my father's brother-in-law-- I'm sorry, I don't remember the name-- my father's brother, Moris Raznov, and his son, Jerry Raznov. This picture was taken in 1946, in June, 1946, in Baltimore, Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from the left to right of my father's brother, Moris Raznov, his son, Jerry Raznov, and his mother. I'm sorry, I don't remember the name. I can't recall. This picture was taken in June, 1946, in Baltimore, Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose mother? Whose mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother of Jerry, or the wife of Moris Raznov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father's sister, Sheina Sore-- her maiden name is Raznov, and Sheina Sore's husband. I don't-- I'm sorry. I don't remember the name. This was taken in June, 1946, in Baltimore, Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of partisans from Linz, Austria. This is when I was in Bad Gastein in the teepee camp. And we went with my husband to visit the partisans from Linz, Austria. We took this picture in Linz, Austria. And this picture was taken approximately in 1947 in Linz, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of, from left to right, my husband, Nochem Scelubski, myself, Mira Scelubski, and a partisan, Honia Kletsky. This picture was taken Bad Gastein, Austria, approximately in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the leader of the partisan group in Linz, Austria. His name was Levine, and next to him is his wife. This picture of was taken approximately in 1947 in Linz-- but in Linz, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew, but I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of partisans marching on the streets in Bad Gastein, Austria, demanding the independence of Israel. That was the time when I and my husband, Mira Shelub and Nochem-- Mira Scelubski and Nochem Scelubski, when we lived in Bad Gastein.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1947 in Bad Gastein, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of partisans marching again on the streets in Bad Gastein. As you can see a flag with the letters [NON-ENGLISH] That means, we demand independence for Israel. WIR is W-I-R. AND the pole hits my husband. My husband is right next to the flag, Nochem Scelubski. This picture with a mustache. My husband has a mustache, so you'll be able to recognize him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Under the word?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the word WIR-- we-- W-I-R. This picture was taken in 1947 in Bad Gastein, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of workers at the UNRA in Bad Gastein, Austria. The second from the left, that's me, Mira Scelubski, standing next to the American lady for whom we were working. The rest are all coworkers. This picture was taken between 1946 and 1947 in Bad Gastein, Austria. As I remember, this picture was taken before the American lady, Mrs. Evans, left Bad Gastein for America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the other workers' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one man who's standing right next to me--

speaker: interviewer
text: To, from the left to the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left to the right, Marian Sandel--

speaker: interviewer
text: He's in the-- how many from the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: The third from the left, Marian Sandel. The rest, I'm sorry, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself, Mira Scelubski, with some friends. The second from the right is Sonia Pshenica. We are taking sewing lessons. And we are sitting all together. This picture was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was taken in 1947 in Bad Gastein, Austria, at the displaced persons camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the first from the-- in the first row-- from left to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're number one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a-- is a picture of my whole family. This picture was taken in 1994. That was the time when I graduated from San Francisco State University, and I got a Master's in counseling. The first is my daughter-- the first from the left to the right is my daughter, Elaine Shelub. The second is my daughter-in-law, Sheryl Shelub. The third, that's me, the graduate. The fourth-- Mira Scelubski Shelub. The fourth is my son with the beard. That's my son, Irwin Shelub, who lives in San Mateo. He's practicing medicine. The fourth is my grandson, my son's son, Aaron Shelub. The fifth is my son, Mark Shelub, and the sixth is my grandson, Nathan Shelub. This picture was taking-- taken in 1994, on the campus San Francisco State University.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. I've said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a Fammy Award given to me in 1995 by the Jewish Family and Children's Service for dedicated service for our community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.
